skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render videos from React/Remotion component code via inference.sh. Pass TSX code, get MP4. Supports all Remotion APIs: useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence. Configurable resolution, FPS, duration, codec. Use for: programmatic video generation, animated graphics, motion design, data-driven videos, React animations to video. Triggers: remotion, render video from code, tsx to video, react video, programmatic video, remotion render, code to video, animated video, motion graphics code, react animation video
skill.summary: Programmatic video rendering from React/Remotion TSX code via the inference.sh belt CLI.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: The skill is a single Markdown documentation file (SKILL.md) that teaches users how to invoke the `belt` CLI to render videos from React/Remotion TSX code through the inference.sh API. The 32 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' static findings are false positives: the backticks are Markdown inline code spans (e.g., `npx skills add belt-sh/cli`) and link syntax, not shell command execution. The 6 'Hardcoded URL' findings are documentation links to inference.sh, Remotion, and GitHub raw content, all expected for a CLI integration guide. The 3 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings appear to be scanner misfires on unrelated text in lines 3 and 35; no actual cryptography is present. The front-matter declares `allowed-tools: Bash(belt *)`, which narrowly scopes shell access to the `belt` CLI only, a good security practice. No prompt injection, no obfuscation, and no data exfiltration patterns were found.

low_findings.title: Network calls to inference.sh API
low_findings.description: The skill instructs users to invoke `belt app run infsh/remotion-render`, which sends the user's React/Remotion TSX code to the inference.sh remote API for rendering. This is the documented and intended functionality, but it does transmit user-authored code over the network to a third-party service.

low_findings.title: Bash tool access scoped to belt CLI
low_findings.description: The skill declares `allowed-tools: Bash(belt *)`, which permits any `belt` subcommand. This is broader than a single subcommand allowlist but is consistent with normal CLI workflows. No evidence of abuse; flagged for transparency.

content.user_title: Render React videos from TSX code in seconds
content.value_statement: Writing a video renderer in code usually means setting up Remotion, bundling React, and waiting on slow local builds. This skill turns React/Remotion components into MP4 files through a single CLI call, so you can ship motion graphics without touching a build pipeline.
actual_capabilities: Render Remotion TSX components to MP4 via the `belt` CLI
Pass full React component code as a string in the `code` parameter
Configure video width, height, FPS, duration, and codec per render
Pass runtime props to the rendered component
Use Remotion APIs: useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence, Audio, Video, Img
Stream rendering progress with the Python SDK
limitations: Requires the user to install and authenticate the inference.sh `belt` CLI
Only renders TSX that imports from `remotion` and `react`; no other npm packages
Render output is hosted by inference.sh; no local offline rendering
No multi-track timeline or audio mixing beyond Remotion's built-in Audio and Video components

use_cases.title: Quick social media clips from React
use_cases.description: A growth marketer wants a short animated promo without opening a video editor. They paste a Remotion TSX snippet and get an MP4 in under a minute.

use_cases.title: Data-driven explainer videos
use_cases.description: A developer building product tutorials feeds in a Remotion component that reads props for numbers and labels, then renders a polished explainer with one CLI call.

use_cases.title: Automated motion graphics in CI
use_cases.description: A design systems team runs the `belt` render command in a CI job to regenerate motion assets whenever the design tokens change.

prompt_templates.title: Render a fade-in title card
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the remotion-render skill to render a 2-second title card that fades in. Make the background dark navy and the text 'Welcome' in white, 80px. Output as 1920x1080 at 30 FPS.

prompt_templates.title: Animated progress counter
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the remotion-render skill to render a 5-second loading animation at 1080x1080 and 60 FPS. Show a large percentage counter that goes from 0 to 100 and a small 'Loading...' label below it. Use interpolate for the easing.

prompt_templates.title: Spring-bounce logo intro
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the remotion-render skill to render a 2-second logo intro at 1080x1080 and 60 FPS. A white 200x200 rounded square should bounce in using spring with damping 10 and stiffness 100. Background color should be a purple #6366f1.

prompt_templates.title: Props-driven video with three sections
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the remotion-render skill to render a 4-second 1920x1080 sequence at 30 FPS. Use three Sequence blocks that fade in 'First', 'Second', 'Third' on a black background, each 1 second apart, with the title passed in as a prop from input.

output_examples.input: User asks Claude to render a 3-second 'Hello World' fade-in title card at 1920x1080, 30 FPS.
output_examples.output: Claude invokes `belt app run infsh/remotion-render` with the TSX code and parameters, then returns a hosted MP4 URL the user can preview, download, or post on social channels.

output_examples.input: User asks Claude to render a spring-bounce logo intro with purple background, 2 seconds at 1080x1080, 60 FPS.
output_examples.output: Claude builds the Remotion component with `spring({ frame, fps, config: { damping: 10, stiffness: 100 } })`, runs the render, and reports the output video URL along with render time.

best_practices: Keep TSX code self-contained; only import from `remotion` and `react` so the remote renderer can resolve it.
Use `interpolate` and `spring` for smooth motion rather than raw `useCurrentFrame` math.
Set explicit `width`, `height`, `fps`, and `duration_seconds` for predictable render output.
anti_patterns: Pasting TSX that imports third-party npm packages; the remote renderer only supports `remotion` and `react`.
Hard-coding secrets or credentials into the component code, which would be sent to the inference.sh API.
Asking for ultra-long renders (over a few minutes) without streaming the progress to confirm the job is alive.

faq.question: What is Remotion?
faq.answer: Remotion is a framework for creating videos programmatically using React components. It renders each frame from a React tree, so you can use familiar React patterns to build motion graphics.

faq.question: Do I need to install Remotion locally?
faq.answer: No. The skill sends your TSX code to the inference.sh render service, which runs the render remotely. You only need the `belt` CLI and an inference.sh account.

faq.question: Can I import npm packages other than `remotion` and `react`?
faq.answer: No. The remote renderer only resolves the `remotion` and `react` packages. Any other import will fail at render time.

faq.question: How is the output video delivered?
faq.answer: The render job returns a hosted URL on inference.sh where you can preview or download the resulting MP4.

faq.question: Can I stream render progress?
faq.answer: Yes. The Python SDK supports a streaming mode that yields progress updates as the render proceeds, which is useful for long renders.

faq.question: Does the skill send my code anywhere?
faq.answer: Yes. The TSX code is sent to the inference.sh API as part of the render request. Do not embed secrets in the component code.
